metadata.summary: Enforce Conventional Commits 1.0.0 via a commitlint commit-msg hook and CI PR-title check so changelogs and semver tags are generated deterministically.

content.title: Conventional Commits Enforced at the Hook
content.summary: Every repo enforces Conventional Commits 1.0.0 (`feat`, `fix`, `chore`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `perf`, `build`, `ci`, `style`, `revert`; optional scope; optional `!` for breaking; mandatory `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer when `!` is used) via a `commitlint` `commit-msg` hook plus a CI PR-title check. The CHANGELOG, semver bump, and release notes are derived deterministically from the log; agents never freeform commit subjects. Non-conformant messages are rejected at hook time, before they reach the remote.

why.p: AI agents produce many small commits. Without a hard format the log degrades into "updates", "fixes", "wip" within a sprint, and downstream tooling (release-please, semantic-release, conventional-changelog) cannot infer the next version or generate release notes. Conventional Commits gives the LLM a closed grammar with a small enum and a tiny set of footers, which is the format LLMs follow most reliably; commitlint catches the rare miss before push. The result is a deterministic pipeline from commit message to changelog to npm/PyPI release tag.

list.item: Any team repo that produces releases (libraries, services with semver, monorepos with independent package versions).
Repos where AI agents create commits autonomously and a human curator does not edit every subject line.
Projects with downstream consumers expecting semver-correct changelogs (npm, PyPI, NuGet, Maven Central, Helm charts).
Monorepos using `release-please` / `semantic-release` / `changesets` — these tools refuse to operate without conformant messages.
Single-developer scratch repos and throwaway prototypes — overhead exceeds benefit.
Mirror / vendored repos where commits are imported verbatim from upstream.
Migration-in-progress repos where rewriting historical messages is out of scope; turn it on at a fresh tag and skip backfill.
Repos that intentionally use squash-on-merge with auto-generated subjects from PR titles — gate the PR title instead.

section.name: grammar-and-hook
section.p: Reject non-conformant commit messages at the `commit-msg` hook with `commitlint`; enforce the same rule on PR titles in CI.

Conventional Commits 1.0.0 defines a header `<type>(<scope>)?!: <subject>` where `type` is from a closed enum. A `commit-msg` hook running `commitlint` rejects deviations before the commit object is created.

rule.statement: Every repo MUST install a `commit-msg` hook (lefthook / husky / pre-commit) that runs `commitlint --edit $1`. The allowed types are `feat`, `fix`, `chore`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `perf`, `build`, `ci`, `style`, `revert`. Subject MUST be in imperative mood, MUST NOT end with a period, MUST be at most 72 characters. Breaking changes MUST use `!` after the type/scope AND a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. The same rule MUST run in CI on the PR title via `commitlint-github-action` (or platform equivalent) so squash merges keep the contract. Agents are forbidden from `--no-verify` to bypass the hook; if the hook fails, fix the message.
rule.rationale: A closed grammar enforced at the hook is the cheapest deterministic anchor available — every downstream tool (changelog, semver bump, release notes) can pattern-match the log without an LLM, and agents stop drifting toward freeform subjects.

example.title: Breaking change with body and footer
example.code:
feat(billing)!: switch invoices to two-decimal rounding
The legacy half-up rounding produced cents-off totals on EUR. Mitigation: read `BILLING_LEGACY_ROUNDING=1` for one release.
BREAKING CHANGE: invoice totals change for in-flight subscriptions. Refs: ENG-1873
example.note: Type, scope, `!` for breaking, body explains why, footer references the tracker.

example.title: Freeform commit subject
example.note: No type, no scope, no imperative; release-please cannot infer the bump and the changelog entry is empty.

antipattern.description: Agent runs `git commit --no-verify -m "fix"` to avoid a failing commitlint check.
antipattern.reason: The hook bypass lands a commit the changelog tool cannot parse; the next release silently drops the fix from the notes.

section.name: changelog-and-release
section.p: Use release-please / semantic-release to derive CHANGELOG entries and semver tags directly from the conformant commit log; never hand-edit.

Once the grammar is locked, a release tool reads the merged commit subjects, infers the next semver, and rewrites `CHANGELOG.md`. Hand-editing the changelog or the version field defeats the determinism.

rule.statement: Repos MUST drive releases via one of `release-please`, `semantic-release`, or `changesets`, configured to read Conventional Commits. The mapping MUST be `feat` → minor, `fix` / `perf` → patch, `BREAKING CHANGE:` → major; chore/docs/test/style/build/ci do NOT bump version. `CHANGELOG.md` MUST be a generated artifact under `## [Unreleased]` until the release tool promotes the section. Agents MUST NOT hand-write version numbers in `package.json` / `pyproject.toml` / `Cargo.toml` and MUST NOT hand-edit released sections of `CHANGELOG.md`. The Unreleased section is the only place agents touch.
rule.rationale: Hand-edits compound: one missing fix in the changelog teaches the agent that the file is editable; six months later the release notes diverge from the log and consumers cannot trust the version.

example.title: release-please GitHub Actions workflow
example.code: # .github/workflows/release-please.yml on: push: branches: [main] permissions: contents: write pull-requests: write jobs: release: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: googleapis/release-please-action@v4 with: release-type: python package-name: my-pkg
example.note: release-please opens a `release-pr` summarizing the changelog; merging it tags and publishes.

example.title: Agent hand-edits version and CHANGELOG
example.code: # Agent hand-edits CHANGELOG.md and version manually: git commit -m "chore: bump to 1.4.0"
example.note: The release tool now sees `chore:` (no bump) but the version moved; future runs report the wrong "since" range.

antipattern.description: Manually adding a "## 1.4.0" section to CHANGELOG.md to ship a hotfix faster, instead of merging a `fix:` PR.
antipattern.reason: The next release-please run will collide with the manual section and either skip the entry or reopen it; the audit trail breaks.

template.purpose: commitlint rule set with extended Conventional Commits and faion-net types.
`commit-msg` hook running commitlint without npx round-trip.
